original_abstract: Epoxides serve as critical intermediates in the synthesis of pharmaceuticals and epoxy resins. In this work, we report a highly efficient photoelectrochemical epoxidation system utilizing a Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª mediator on an Œ±-Fe‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ photoanode. By using water as the oxygen source, this system achieves exceptional performance across a broad range of alkenes, reaching selectivities exceeding 99% and faradaic efficiencies up to 82 ¬± 4%‚Äîresults that significantly outperform previously reported electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods. Mechanistic investigations confirm that the process follows a Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª mediated pathway; specifically, Br‚Åª undergoes non-radical oxidation to BrO‚Åª via oxygen atom transfer on the Œ±-Fe‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ surface, followed by a subsequent oxygen transfer from BrO‚Åª to the alkene substrate. The combination of this non-radical mechanism and favorable thermodynamics ensures high catalytic efficiency. This Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª-mediated photoelectrochemical approach offers a sustainable and promising strategy for the simultaneous production of high-value epoxides and green hydrogen.
humanized_abstract: Here, we present a highly efficient photoelectrochemical epoxidation catalyst system for diverse alkenes, employing Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª as redox mediator on an Œ±-Fe‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ photoanode and water as an oxygen source. The Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª mediator system affords a high selectivity (selectivities > 99%) and excellent faradaic efficiencies (up to 82 ¬± 4 %) that far exceed all previous reported electrochemical and photoelectrochemical studies. By identification of a Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª mediated mechanism with non-radical oxygen atom transfer (OAT) to oxidize Br‚Åª to BrO‚Åª on the photoanode and subsequent selective transfer of O from BrO‚Åª to the unsaturated bond of alkenes, this Br‚Åª/BrO‚Åª mediated photoelectrochemical system provides an environmentally friendly pathway to simultaneously produce valuable-oxidized products, such as desired chemical products of high commercial value, like various type of epoxides, along with green hydrogen, as a renewable energy carrier.
